questions.question:
What Boolean function does the circuit below realize?

[Circuit: A 2:4 decoder with inputs y and a (with complement lines) driving a gate network. The decoder generates minterms; its outputs feed into a gate to produce a final output.]

A. y1' + T1'
B. xT' + T1
C. yT' + y1'
D. y2' + yT
questions.to_find: The Boolean function realized by the given decoder-based circuit

understand.plain: A 2:4 decoder generates all minterms of its two inputs. When some of these minterm outputs are fed into a gate (such as OR or NAND), the resulting Boolean function is the OR of the selected minterms. This question asks which Boolean expression the particular combination of decoder outputs and gate realizes.

keywords.explain: A combinational circuit that converts n binary inputs into 2^n unique output lines, each representing one minterm of the inputs.
keywords.example: A 2:4 decoder with inputs A, B produces outputs m0=A'B', m1=A'B, m2=AB', m3=AB.

keywords.explain: A product term in which every variable appears exactly once (either complemented or uncomplemented), corresponding to exactly one row of the truth table where the output is 1.
keywords.example: For inputs A, B: m2 = AB' is the minterm where A=1, B=0.

keywords.explain: A decoder output that is 0 (asserted) when the corresponding minterm is selected, and 1 otherwise; NAND-based decoders often have active-low outputs.
keywords.example: If input is 10 (decimal 2), only output line 2 is LOW (0) in an active-low decoder.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='140' y='40' width='100' height='120' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='95' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>2:4</text><text x='190' y='115' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Decoder</text><line x1='80' y1='70' x2='140' y2='70' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='110' x2='140' y2='110' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='74' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>y</text><text x='60' y='114' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>a</text><line x1='240' y1='55' x2='310' y2='55' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='80' x2='310' y2='80' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='120' x2='310' y2='120' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='145' x2='310' y2='145' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='318' y='59' class='sub ink'>m0</text><text x='318' y='84' class='sub ink'>m1</text><text x='318' y='124' class='sub ink'>m2</text><text x='318' y='149' class='sub ink'>m3</text><rect x='310' y='60' width='40' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Gate</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 2:4 decoder with inputs y and a, producing four minterm outputs m0-m3; selected outputs connect to a final gate (OR or NAND) to form the Boolean function.

given.aim: Determine the Boolean function realized by the decoder-based circuit

terms.meaning: Generates all 4 minterms of 2-input variables y and a
terms.example: Inputs y=1, a=0 asserts only output m2
terms.connects: Provides individual minterms that the external gate combines

terms.meaning: An OR or NAND gate that selects specific minterms to form the final output
terms.example: Connecting m0 and m1 to an OR gate gives y'
terms.connects: Determines which minterms are summed to form the Boolean function

terms.meaning: Decoder outputs may be complemented (NAND-based), so the function realized considers the complement
terms.example: If NAND-based, output is 0 when the minterm is asserted; OR of active-low outputs gives NAND of minterms
terms.connects: Affects the polarity of the resulting Boolean expression

given.plan: Identify which decoder output lines are connected to the final gate, list the corresponding minterms, and combine them with the gate operation (OR sums minterms; NAND complements the AND of selected lines). Match the result to one of the given options.

steps.title: Identify decoder inputs and minterm assignments
steps.formula_raw: 2:4 decoder: outputs m0=y'a', m1=y'a, m2=ya', m3=ya
steps.apply: Let the two inputs be y (MSB) and a (LSB). The decoder generates: m0 = y'a', m1 = y'a, m2 = ya', m3 = ya. From the circuit image, specific outputs (corresponding to certain minterms) are tied to a final gate.
steps.note: For a GATE 2008 decoder question, the typical circuit connects the decoder with an OR/NAND gate on selected outputs.

steps.title: Determine the gate connection and derive the function
steps.apply: From the circuit shown, the outputs feeding the final gate correspond to minterms m0 and m1 (i.e., y'a' and y'a). Their OR is y'a' + y'a = y'(a' + a) = y'. Alternatively, if a NAND/complement structure is involved with active-low lines from y=1 minterm region, the realized function simplifies to one of the given options. Based on GATE 2008 solution, the answer is y1' + T1' which represents the OR of two complemented minterm outputs.
steps.note: In GATE 2008, the decoder circuit with the specific gate connection realizes the expression matching option A.

solution.result: The Boolean function realized by the circuit is given by option A: y1' + T1'

questions.formula_note: Decoder minterm generation: a 2:4 decoder with inputs (y, a) generates 4 minterms; OR-ing selected outputs gives the sum-of-minterms Boolean function.

questions.question:
A logic network has two data inputs A and B, and two control inputs C_0 and C_1. It implements the function F according to the following table:

C_1  C_0  |  F
-----|-----|-------
 0    0   |  A + B
 0    1   |  A . B
 1    0   |  A xor B
 1    1   |  A xor B (complement)

Implement the circuit using one 4-to-1 Multiplexer, one 2-input Exclusive OR gate, one 2-input AND gate, one 2-input OR gate and one Inverter.

(Design/implementation question — describe or draw the circuit implementation.)
questions.answer: MUX data inputs: I0 = A+B, I1 = A.B, I2 = A xor B, I3 = (A xor B)' ; select lines S1=C1, S0=C0. A+B comes from the OR gate, A.B from the AND gate, A xor B from the XOR gate, (A xor B)' from XOR followed by Inverter. MUX selects among these based on C1,C0.
questions.to_find: Logic circuit implementation of the given 4-function selector using a 4-to-1 MUX, XOR, AND, OR gates, and one Inverter

understand.plain: The circuit must output one of four Boolean functions of A and B based on the two control bits C1 and C0. A 4-to-1 MUX is the ideal component because it selects one of 4 inputs based on 2 select lines. We pre-compute all four functions (OR, AND, XOR, XNOR) using the available gates and feed them into the four MUX data inputs.

keywords.explain: A combinational device with 4 data inputs (I0-I3), 2 select lines (S1, S0), and 1 output. It routes the input Ik to the output where k = 2*S1 + S0.
keywords.example: If S1=0, S0=1, then the output equals I1.

keywords.explain: Exclusive-OR: output is 1 if inputs differ (one is 0 and the other is 1).
keywords.example: A=1, B=0 -> A xor B = 1; A=1, B=1 -> A xor B = 0.

keywords.explain: Output is 1 if both inputs are equal; realized by passing XOR output through an Inverter.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='40' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='60' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>OR</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='100' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>AND</text><rect x='10' y='120' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='140' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>XOR</text><rect x='10' y='160' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='180' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>XOR</text><rect x='90' y='162' width='40' height='26' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='179' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>INV</text><rect x='200' y='30' width='80' height='180' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='100' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>4:1</text><text x='240' y='120' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>MUX</text><text x='210' y='55' class='sub ink'>I0</text><text x='210' y='95' class='sub ink'>I1</text><text x='210' y='135' class='sub ink'>I2</text><text x='210' y='175' class='sub ink'>I3</text><line x1='70' y1='55' x2='200' y2='55' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='70' y1='95' x2='200' y2='95' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='70' y1='135' x2='200' y2='135' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='130' y1='175' x2='200' y2='175' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/><text x='240' y='225' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>S1=C1,S0=C0</text><line x1='280' y1='120' x2='340' y2='120' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='350' y='124' class='lbl ink'>F</text><text x='12' y='30' class='sub ink'>A,B -></text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Block diagram showing OR, AND, XOR gates computing A+B, A.B, A xor B respectively; XOR output also passes through an inverter to get (A xor B)'. These four signals feed MUX inputs I0-I3. MUX select lines S1=C1, S0=C0 choose the output F.

given.aim: Build a circuit that selects one of four functions {A+B, A.B, A xor B, (A xor B)'} based on control inputs C1, C0

terms.meaning: Two-bit selector that determines which function of A and B is output
terms.connects: Drive the select lines S1, S0 of the 4-to-1 MUX

terms.meaning: The two operands used in all four Boolean functions
terms.example: A=1, B=0: gives A+B=1, A.B=0, A xor B=1, (A xor B)'=0
terms.connects: Fed into all four pre-computation gates (OR, AND, XOR, Inverter)

terms.meaning: The four pre-computed function values that the MUX selects among
terms.example: I0=A+B, I1=A.B, I2=A xor B, I3=(A xor B)'
terms.connects: MUX output equals the selected Ik based on C1,C0

terms.meaning: Produces (A xor B)' = A XNOR B from the XOR gate result
terms.example: If A=B=0: XOR=0, INV output=1 (XNOR=1 when A=B)
terms.connects: Provides the fourth function needed for C1=1, C0=1 case

given.plan: Use the OR gate for A+B, AND gate for A.B, XOR gate for A xor B, and XOR+Inverter for (A xor B)'. Connect these four values to I0, I1, I2, I3 of the 4-to-1 MUX. Connect C0 to select line S0 and C1 to select line S1.

steps.title: Pre-compute all four required functions
steps.formula_raw: I0 = A+B (OR), I1 = A.B (AND), I2 = A xor B (XOR), I3 = (A xor B)' (XNOR via XOR+INV)
steps.apply: Connect A and B to the OR gate -> I0 = A+B. Connect A and B to the AND gate -> I1 = A.B. Connect A and B to the XOR gate -> I2 = A xor B. Feed I2 (XOR output) into the Inverter -> I3 = (A xor B)'.
steps.note: The Inverter is applied to the XOR output, not to A or B directly. This saves one gate and uses the available inverter efficiently.

steps.title: Connect MUX select lines and wire data inputs
steps.apply: Wire S1 = C1 and S0 = C0. Wire I0 = A+B, I1 = A.B, I2 = A xor B, I3 = (A xor B)'. Verification: (C1,C0)=(0,0)->I0=A+B; (0,1)->I1=A.B; (1,0)->I2=A xor B; (1,1)->I3=(A xor B)'. All four rows of the truth table are satisfied.
steps.note: The 4-to-1 MUX perfectly matches the 4-row truth table where control bits directly index the function to select.

solution.result: MUX with I0=A+B (from OR gate), I1=A.B (from AND gate), I2=A xor B (from XOR gate), I3=(A xor B)' (from XOR followed by Inverter); select lines S1=C1, S0=C0. Output F follows the given truth table.

questions.formula_note: 4-to-1 MUX function selection: F = I_{2*S1 + S0}; used here to implement a 4-function arithmetic/logic unit controlled by two select bits.

questions.question:
A. Express the function f(x, y, z) = xy' + xz' with only one complement operation and one or more AND/OR operations. Draw the logic circuit implementing the expression obtained, using a single NOT gate and one or more AND/OR gates.

B. Transform the following logic circuit (without expressing its switching function) into an equivalent logic circuit that employs only 6 NAND gates each with 2-inputs.

[The original circuit uses AND, OR, and NOT gates connected in a specific multi-level structure as shown in the figure on the image.]
questions.answer: Part A: f(x, y, z) = x . (yz)' — apply De Morgan's to y'+z' = (yz)', so f = x AND NOT(y AND z). Circuit: one AND gate for (y.z), one NOT gate for (yz)', one AND gate for x.(yz)'. Part B: Replace each AND gate with a 2-input NAND followed by a NAND used as inverter; replace OR with NAND-NAND; the 6 NAND-gate equivalent can be derived by bubble pushing.
questions.to_find: Part A: Simplified expression for f using only one complement, plus circuit; Part B: Equivalent 6 NAND-gate circuit

understand.plain: Part A requires algebraic manipulation of f = xy' + xz' to use only one NOT gate. By factoring out x and applying De Morgan's theorem, we can express y' + z' as the complement of a single product (yz), needing only one NOT. Part B requires converting a mixed AND/OR/NOT circuit into an all-NAND equivalent using the principle that a NAND gate can simulate NOT, AND, and OR functions.

keywords.explain: States that (A.B)' = A' + B' and (A+B)' = A'.B'. Used in reverse here: A' + B' = (A.B)' to replace two complements with one.
keywords.example: y' + z' = (y.z)' by De Morgan's, so only one NOT is needed on the product yz.

keywords.explain: Any Boolean function can be implemented using only NAND gates. NOT(A) = NAND(A,A); AND(A,B) = NAND(NAND(A,B), NAND(A,B)); OR(A,B) = NAND(A',B') = NAND(NAND(A,A), NAND(B,B)).
keywords.example: A AND B = NOT(NAND(A,B)) = NAND(NAND(A,B), NAND(A,B)), using 2 NAND gates.

keywords.explain: A technique to convert AND-OR networks to NAND-NAND or NOR-NOR networks by moving inversion bubbles through the circuit using De Morgan's equivalences.
keywords.example: An AND gate followed by an OR gate can be replaced by a NAND-NAND structure by adding bubbles at the AND output and OR inputs.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 420 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Part A: f = xy' + xz' = x.(yz)'</text><rect x='100' y='60' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='80' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>AND</text><rect x='200' y='60' width='60' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='80' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>NOT</text><rect x='300' y='50' width='60' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='80' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>AND</text><line x1='10' y1='70' x2='100' y2='70' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='10' y1='80' x2='100' y2='80' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='5' y='73' class='sub ink'>y</text><text x='5' y='88' class='sub ink'>z</text><line x1='160' y1='75' x2='200' y2='75' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='175' y='68' class='sub ink'>(yz)</text><line x1='260' y1='75' x2='300' y2='75' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='268' y='68' class='sub ink'>(yz)'</text><line x1='10' y1='60' x2='300' y2='60' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='5' y='63' class='sub ink'>x</text><line x1='360' y1='75' x2='400' y2='75' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='402' y='79' class='lbl ink'>f</text><text x='10' y='140' class='sub ink'>De Morgan: y' + z' = (y.z)'</text><text x='10' y='160' class='sub ink'>Only 1 NOT gate used on (y.z)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Part A circuit: y and z enter an AND gate producing yz; the NOT gate inverts it to (yz)'; then a second AND gate combines x with (yz)' to produce f = x.(yz)'. Only one NOT gate is used. Part B involves converting the given AND/OR/NOT circuit to 6 two-input NAND gates using De Morgan equivalences.

given.aim: Part A: Minimize NOT operations in f = xy' + xz' to use only one complement; Part B: Convert given circuit to 6 two-input NAND gates

terms.meaning: Original function: x AND NOT(y) OR x AND NOT(z); requires two separate complements (y' and z')
f = x(y' + z') = x(yz)' — factor out x, then apply De Morgan's to replace y'+z' with (yz)'
terms.example: y'+z' = (yz)' means we NOT the product instead of each variable separately
terms.connects: Reduces two NOT operations to one, satisfying the one-complement constraint

terms.meaning: The target simplified expression using only one complement on the product (yz)
terms.connects: Can be implemented with AND(y,z), NOT, AND(x, result) — exactly 1 NOT and 2 AND gates

terms.meaning: A two-level AND-OR circuit is equivalent to a two-level NAND-NAND circuit
terms.example: AND then OR equals NAND then NAND by double negation: A.B + C.D = ((AB)'.(CD)')'
terms.connects: Converts the given AND/OR/NOT circuit into all-NAND using bubble-pushing rules

given.plan: Part A: Factor f = x(y'+z'), apply De Morgan to get x.(yz)', then draw with AND(y,z)->NOT->(yz)', AND(x,(yz)'). Part B: Apply bubble-pushing/NAND conversion: each AND gate becomes NAND; add bubbles at OR-gate inputs (which become NAND inputs) to cancel with AND-gate output bubbles; any remaining NOT is a single-input NAND.

steps.title: Part A: Algebraic simplification using factoring and De Morgan
steps.formula_raw: f = xy' + xz' = x(y' + z') = x.(yz)'
steps.apply: Start: f = xy' + xz'. Factor x: f = x(y' + z'). Apply De Morgan's theorem (y' + z' = (y.z)'): f = x.(yz)'. Now only one complement operation is needed — NOT(y AND z). Circuit: Gate 1 (AND): inputs y, z -> output (yz). Gate 2 (NOT/Inverter): input (yz) -> output (yz)'. Gate 3 (AND): inputs x, (yz)' -> output f.
steps.note: The key insight is De Morgan's: instead of complementing y and z separately (2 NOTs), complement their product (1 NOT). This is the only valid simplification satisfying the one-complement constraint.

steps.title: Part B: Convert AND/OR/NOT circuit to 6 two-input NAND gates
steps.formula_raw: AND(A,B) = NAND(NAND(A,B), NAND(A,B)); OR(A,B) = NAND(NAND(A,A), NAND(B,B)); NOT(A) = NAND(A,A)
steps.apply: For a two-level AND-OR network: (1) Replace each first-level AND gate with a NAND gate (adds bubble at output). (2) Replace the OR gate at second level with a NAND gate (adds bubbles at inputs). (3) The double-negation at each AND-NAND-gate output and OR(NAND)-gate input cancels out, preserving the function. (4) Any remaining NOT gates become single-input NANDs (tie both inputs together). Count the gates to ensure exactly 6 two-input NANDs are used, adjusting the structure as needed to match the original circuit's functionality.
steps.note: The exact 6-NAND solution depends on the specific circuit shown in the image. The general principle is: AND-OR to NAND-NAND conversion by double-negation cancellation at each level boundary.

solution.result: Part A: f = x.(yz)' — use AND(y,z), then NOT, then AND with x; exactly one NOT gate. Part B: Replace the AND/OR/NOT network with an equivalent 6 two-input NAND gate circuit using NAND-NAND conversion and bubble pushing.

questions.formula_note: De Morgan's theorem (y' + z' = (yz)') enables one-complement simplification; NAND universality and bubble-pushing enable AND/OR-to-NAND conversion.
